one_liner: A 38-year-old previously healthy woman presents with a 10-day history of persistent cough with mucoid sputum following a recent upper respiratory infection.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient initially developed rhinorrhea, sore throat, and malaise 2 weeks ago, which resolved after 4-5 days. However, she developed a persistent cough that has worsened over the past 10 days. The cough is productive of clear to yellowish sputum. She reports mild substernal chest discomfort with coughing and occasional wheezing, particularly at night. She denies high fever, rigors, hemoptysis, dyspnea at rest, or pleuritic chest pain. She tried over-the-counter dextromethorphan with minimal relief. She is concerned because the cough has not improved.
stage2.pmh: Seasonal allergic rhinitis. No asthma or chronic lung disease.
stage2.medications: Loratadine 10 mg daily PRN for allergies.
stage2.social_history: Elementary school teacher. Never smoker. Social alcohol on weekends. No occupational exposures. Up-to-date on vaccinations including influenza. Lives with husband and two children.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Well-appearing woman, not in distress, coughing intermittently during exam**. HEENT: **Mild pharyngeal erythema without exudate**; **Nasal mucosa mildly erythematous**. Lungs: **Scattered bilateral rhonchi that partially clear with coughing**; No focal consolidation, no crackles, no wheezing at rest. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. Extremities: No edema.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Normal differential; no left shift to suggest bacterial infection
Mildly elevated but not suggestive of bacterial pneumonia (typically >5-10 in bacterial infection)
Negative; though may be falsely negative late in illness, clinical timing not consistent with acute influenza
Confirms viral etiology — rhinovirus is one of the most common causes of this condition

imaging_results.result: Clear lung fields bilaterally. No infiltrates, consolidation, effusion, or masses. Normal cardiac silhouette.

key_tests: Clinical diagnosis (no routine testing needed in uncomplicated cases)

debrief.classic_presentation: Previously healthy adult with a cough lasting >5 days following URI symptoms, productive of mucoid/yellowish sputum, with scattered rhonchi on exam, normal vital signs, clear chest X-ray, and normal inflammatory markers.
debrief.pathophysiology: Acute bronchitis is inflammation of the tracheobronchial tree, caused by viral infection in >90% of cases (rhinovirus, coronavirus, influenza, parainfluenza, RSV, adenovirus, metapneumovirus). The viral infection causes epithelial damage, mucosal edema, and mucus hypersecretion. Transient bronchial hyperreactivity from epithelial disruption and exposed nerve endings can cause cough that persists for 2-3 weeks (or longer). Purulent sputum does NOT indicate bacterial infection — it reflects neutrophilic inflammation and sloughed epithelial cells.
debrief.treatment_overview: Supportive care: rest, hydration, antipyretics/analgesics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs) for discomfort. Antitussives (dextromethorphan, honey) for symptomatic cough relief. Inhaled bronchodilators (albuterol) may help if significant wheezing is present. Antibiotics are NOT indicated — acute bronchitis is overwhelmingly viral and antibiotics provide no benefit. Procalcitonin-guided strategies can reduce unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions. Counsel patients that cough may persist for 2-3 weeks and is expected. Seek care if worsening dyspnea, high fever, hemoptysis, or symptoms beyond 3 weeks.
high_yield_pearls: Acute bronchitis is the most common condition for which antibiotics are inappropriately prescribed — >70% of patients receive antibiotics despite >90% viral etiology.
Purulent (yellow/green) sputum does NOT indicate bacterial infection and is not an indication for antibiotics.
Procalcitonin <0.1 ng/mL strongly argues against bacterial infection and can be used to safely withhold antibiotics.
Cough from acute bronchitis can last 2-3 weeks (mean duration ~18 days) — set appropriate patient expectations.
A normal chest X-ray in a patient with cough and no focal lung findings on exam effectively rules out pneumonia.
sources: ACCP Guidelines: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Bronchitis, 2006 (reaffirmed)
CDC: Antibiotic Prescribing and Use — Acute Bronchitis
